text:
Admission Date:  2006-07-06       Discharge Date:  2010-03-13

Date of Birth:   1991-12-19       Sex:  F

Service:

CHIEF COMPLAINT:  Right carotid endarterectomy incision,
swelling, and bleeding.

HISTORY OF THE PRESENT ILLNESS:  Robert Higgins is a
70-year-old female who underwent a right carotid
endarterectomy on 1972-08-02 with a patch angioplasty.  She
developed wound bleeding post discharge.  She returned and
underwent exploration with revision of the angioplasty with
greater saphenous vein on 1972-11-06.  Cultures, at that
time, grew Staphylococcus aureus.  She was placed on
Vancomycin, and she was discharged home on Vancomycin IV.
Today, she presents with bloody drainage and swelling at the
neck incision site.  The patient returned to
.................... where she was evaluated and electively
intubated and transferred here for urgent neck exploration.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Hypertension.
2.  Carotid disease.
3.  Coronary artery disease, status post MI.
4.  History of noninsulin dependent diabetes mellitus.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:  Previous surgeries include the
following:  Right carotid endarterectomy, coronary artery
bypass graft in 1982 and open cholecystectomy.

ADMISSION MEDICATIONS:
1.  Lopressor 25 mg b.i.d.
2.  Aspirin 325 q.d.
3.  ....................q.d.
4.  Lopid 600 mg b.i.d.
5.  NPH insulin.

ALLERGIES:  The patient is allergic to ZESTRIL and
ZESTORETIC, which causes anaphylaxis.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  The patient is awake, sedated, but
opens eyes spontaneously when asked questions.  Pupils are
equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation.  Right
neck dressing is intact.  Endotracheal tube is in place.
Respirations are clear to auscultation bilaterally.  Heart
revealed regular rate and rhythm.  Abdomen is benign.
Extremities are warm.  She has intact pulse.  Neurologically,
she is intact.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  The patient was admitted to the hospital.
She underwent urgent right neck exploration with evacuation
of hematoma.  The patch was examined.  There was no
disruption or infection noted.  She tolerated the procedure
well.  She was transferred to the SICU in stable condition.
Postoperative hematocrit was 32.3.  The BUN was 22,
creatinine 0.8, potassium 4.1, PT/INR and PTT were normal.

She was placed on nitroglycerin to maintain a systolic blood
pressure of less than 150.

On postoperative day #1, there were no overnight events and
the nitroglycerin was weaned off.  The patient was continued
on Vancomycin and given gentamicin perioperatively.  She was
continued on her pre-admission medications.

Culture was obtained of the endarterectomy site, which gram
stain showed 2+ PMNs, but no organisms.  Infectious Disease
was requested to re-evaluate the patient and determine total
length of therapy.  Recommendations were to continue present
IV antibiotic therapy.  She did not need gram negative
coverage or double coverage for MRSA.  Blood cultures were
obtained, which showed no growth.  The patient was extubated
on postoperative day #1.  The patient was transferred to the
VICU for continued monitoring and care.

On postoperative day #2, there were no overnight events.  The
patient's blood pressure remained stable.  She was allowed
out of bed.  POs were begun.  Insulin was re-instituted.

On postoperative day #3, she continued to do well.  Neck
examination remained unremarkable.  The Vancomycin dosing was
adjusted for peak and trough.  The Allergy Department was
requested to give us an opinion for evaluation for her
Oxacillin allergy.  They felt that the skin testing would not
give us any more information than we already had.  So, the
patient was continued on the Vancomycin.  It was noted on
postoperative day #8 a bump in her creatinine to a peak of
1.8.  She has serial creatinines monitored.  She required IV
hydration with minimal or little change in her creatinine.
Renal Service was requested to see the patient on 1997-10-24.
Their impression and recommendations were as follows:
Creatinine was obtained, which was elevated, which was 2.2,
which was consistent with hypoperfusion.  It was felt that
she had an Williams syndrome, which was consistent with
nephritis secondary to Staphylococcus infection.
Recommendations were to discontinue all unnecessary
medications, re-adjust her antihypertensives, discontinue
Hydralazine, begin her on Miller and beta blockers, and serially
monitor the BUN and creatinine.  I do not feel that the
Vancomycin was the etiology of her creatinine bump.

The patient remained in the hospital under the creatinine
showed improvement.  She was discharged to home in stable
condition.  She will continue on her antibiotics for a total
of four to six weeks from the initial onset.  She should
followup with Dr. Wilson on a p.r.n. basis, or if there
were any wound changes.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
1.  Vancomycin 500 mg q.24h.
2.  Diovan 80 mg q.d.
3.  Lopressor 100 mg b.i.d.
4.  NPH Insulin, q.a.m.
5.  Insulin sliding scale before meals as follows:  0 to 200,
no insulin; 201 to 250, three units; 251 to 300, five units;
301 to 350, seven units; 351 to 400, nine units; greater than
400 Deborah Montgomery MD MD, give eleven units.
6.  Lopid 600 mg q.d.
7.  Tylenol 650 mg q.4-6h.p.r.n.pain.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Right neck hematoma status post exploration and
evacuation.
2.  Hypertension, controlled.
3.  ATN secondary to Staphylococcus infection, improved.




                            [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) **] Perkins, M.D.  68139
